title: East of the Sun and West of the Moon
author: Peter Christen Asbjørnsen & Jørgen Moe

content.text: Once upon a time, in a valley deep within the snow-capped mountains of the far North, there lived a poor peasant who struggled to feed his many children.
Among them, the youngest daughter was so breathtakingly beautiful that her radiance seemed to warm the coldest winter night.
One evening, as a fierce storm howled outside their humble cottage, a loud knocking shook the heavy wooden door.
The father opened it to find a massive, magnificent White Bear standing in the swirling snow. The bear did not growl; instead, he spoke in a deep, gentle voice: "Give me your youngest
daughter, and I will make your family as rich as you are now poor." Though hesitant to part with his beloved child, the father relented when the girl bravely volunteered to go, wanting to
save her family from starvation. The White Bear carried the girl on his back, climbing high into the treacherous mountain passes.
At last, they arrived at a sheer rock face that parted magically to reveal a hidden, glorious palace of gold and crystal.
Inside, the rooms were filled with warmth, soft music, and tables laden with the finest feasts. The bear told her that everything was hers to command, but he made her promise
one thing: she must never attempt to look upon him in the dark. That night, as she lay in her luxurious bed, the bear entered the room, having shed his animal skin in the blackness, and
slept quietly by her side as a man. For months, this pattern continued. She lived in absolute comfort by day, and by night, she felt the presence of a gentle, silent companion, though she
never saw his face. Over time, a deep loneliness crept into the girl's heart, and she begged to visit her family. The White Bear agreed, but warned her: "Your mother will try to give you
advice, but do not listen to her, or you will bring ruin upon us both." When she arrived home, her family rejoiced at her wealth and safety.
However, just as the bear had predicted, the mother grew deeply concerned when the girl confessed she had never seen her nighttime companion.
"He could be a hideous troll!" her mother whispered, handing her a stub of a tallow candle. "Light this tonight while he sleeps, and see what lies in your bed."
Upon returning to the mountain palace, the girl's curiosity overpowered her promise. In the dead of night, while her companion slept deeply, she quietly lit the candle.
Leaning over the bed, she did not see a monster, but the most handsome prince she had ever beheld, with golden hair and a face of pure nobility.
Overcome with love and awe, her hand trembled, and three drops of hot tallow fell from the candle onto his pristine white shirt.
The prince awoke instantly, his eyes filling with profound sorrow. "What have you done?" he cried. "My stepmother cursed me to be a bear by day and a man by night.
Had you only waited a year, the spell would have broken. But now, I must return to her castle, which lies East of the Sun and West of the Moon, and marry a troll princess with a nose
three yards long!" Before she could beg for forgiveness, a violent clap of thunder shook the mountain, and the prince, the palace, and the golden gardens vanished into thin air.
The girl woke up in the morning lying in the middle of a cold, barren forest, dressed in her old, ragged clothes.
Desolate but determined to save her prince, she set off on foot, embarking on a seemingly impossible journey to find a place that no map had ever recorded.
She walked for days, her feet bruised and her heart heavy, asking every beast and leaf for directions, but none had ever heard of the castle East of the Sun and West of the Moon.
In her desperation, she sought out the four winds of the earth. First, she climbed to the peak where the East Wind resided.
Though gentle, he shook his head; he had blown across many lands but had never reached that mysterious castle. He carried her to his brother, the West Wind, but he too had no knowledge of
it. Next, the South Wind took her upon his warm wings and flew over vast deserts and oceans, yet even he had never crossed its path.
Finally, they sought the aid of the oldest and strongest of them all: the mighty, icy North Wind. "It is a long, deadly flight," the North Wind roared, his breath freezing the trees below.
"But I did blow a lone leaf there once, many years ago. Climb upon my back, child, and hold on with all your strength!" The flight was terrifying.
The North Wind raged across frozen oceans and jagged peaks, battling fierce storms that threatened to tear them apart.
Exhausted and near death, the North Wind finally collapsed onto the shore of a dark, misty land. "I can go no further," he gasped.
Before them, silhouetted against a pale sky, stood a towering castle of black stone. The girl thanked the brave wind and approached the gates, where she found the troll stepmother
and her hideous, long-nosed daughter. To gain entry, the girl traded the magical golden items she had been gifted by wise old women along her journey, eventually earning the right
to see the prince. But the stepmother had drugged the prince with a sleeping potion, and the first two nights, the girl's weeping and pleas fell on deaf ears.
On the third night, a sympathetic servant warned the prince, who poured the sleeping draught away. When the girl entered, he embraced her joyfully.
Together, they devised a plan. The next morning, the prince announced to the assembled trolls that he would only marry the woman who could wash the three tallow stains out of his
white shirt. The troll princess and her mother eagerly tried, but the moment they rubbed the fabric, the stains turned black and grew larger. Let the beggar girl try!" the prince declared.
The stepmother sneered, but as soon as the girl dipped the shirt into the pure water, the tallow stains vanished instantly, leaving the cloth whiter than the fresh snow.
Realizing their curse was broken and their power destroyed, the trolls burst with fury and turned to stone.
The black castle collapsed, and the prince and his brave bride were transported back to their home, where they lived in eternal happiness, guided forever by the courage that
had carried them beyond the sun and the moon.
